content:
# Judges Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_268.jpg, JDG_page_269.jpg

### The Angel at Bochim and the Cycle of Disobedience

1. The angel of the LORD went up from Gilgal to Bochim and said to the Israelites, "I brought you out of Egypt into this land that I swore to give your ancestors. I said, 'I will never break my covenant with you,

2. and you must not make any covenants with the people living in this land. Instead, you must tear down their altars.' But you disobeyed my command. Why did you do this?

3. So now I declare that I will no longer drive out the people living in your land. They will be thorns in your sides, and their gods will be a constant temptation to you."

4. When the angel of the LORD finished speaking to all the Israelites, the people wept loudly.

5. So they called that place Bochim (which means "weeping"), and they offered sacrifices to the LORD there.

6. After Joshua sent the people away, each of the tribes left to take possession of the land allotted to them.

7. The Israelites served the LORD throughout the lifetime of Joshua and of the elders who outlived him — those who had personally witnessed all the great things the LORD had done for Israel.

8. Joshua son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died at the age of 110.

9. He was buried in the land he had been given at Timnath-heres, in the hill country of Ephraim, north of Mount Gaash.

10. After that entire generation died, another generation grew up who knew neither the LORD nor what he had done for Israel.

11. The Israelites did evil in the LORD's sight and served the images of Baal.

12. They abandoned the LORD, the God of their ancestors, who had brought them out of Egypt. They went after other gods, worshiping the gods of the people around them. And they angered the LORD.

13. They abandoned the LORD to serve Baal and the images of Ashtoreth.

14. This made the LORD burn with anger against Israel, so he handed them over to raiders who stole their possessions. He turned them over to their enemies all around, and they were no longer able to resist them.

15. Every time Israel went out to battle, the LORD fought against them, causing them to be defeated, just as he had warned. And the people were in great distress.

16. Then the LORD raised up judges to rescue the Israelites from their attackers.

17. Yet Israel did not listen to the judges but prostituted themselves by worshiping other gods, bowing down to them. They quickly abandoned the path that their ancestors had walked — the path of obedience to the LORD's commands. They refused to follow it.

18. Whenever the LORD raised up a judge over Israel, he was with that judge and rescued the people from their enemies throughout the judge's lifetime. For the LORD was moved to pity by the people's groaning under those who oppressed and afflicted them.

19. But when the judge died, the people returned to their corrupt ways, behaving worse than the previous generation, following other gods and serving and worshiping them. They refused to give up any of their evil practices and stubborn ways.

20. So the LORD burned with anger against Israel. He said, "Because these people have violated the covenant I made with their ancestors and have ignored my commands,

21. I will no longer drive out the nations that Joshua left unconquered when he died.

22. I did this to test Israel — to see whether or not they would follow the LORD as their ancestors did."

23. That is why the LORD left those nations in place. He did not quickly drive them out or allow Joshua to conquer them all.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 10 — "Another generation grew up who knew neither the LORD":** This is the key verse of the entire book. One generation's experience of God does not automatically transfer to the next. Each generation must encounter God for themselves. The failure was not intellectual (they knew about God) but experiential (they had not seen his works firsthand).

**The Judges cycle (vv.11-19):** This passage introduces the pattern that will repeat throughout the book: sin → oppression → crying out → deliverance → sin again. But it's not a flat circle — each cycle spirals downward. The people become "more corrupt than their fathers" (v.19). The book of Judges is a story of progressive moral deterioration.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
